intro.overview: Last week you identified 28 vulnerabilities in LoneStar DevOps infrastructure. This week, you’ll transform that raw data into actionable intelligence. Not all vulnerabilities are equal — a CVSS 9.8 on a test server matters less than a CVSS 7.3 on a production database holding customer secrets.

mentorQuote.title: Founder & Principal Consultant, NEXUS Security
mentorQuote.quote: Anyone can run a scanner and produce a list of CVEs. What separates a consultant from a technician is context. A vulnerability report that says ‘fix everything’ is worthless. A report that says ‘fix these three things first because they expose customer data and the enterprise deal depends on it’ — that’s worth the invoice.

objectives: Score all Lab 5.1 vulnerabilities using CVSS 3.1
Map vulnerabilities to business assets and assess impact
Construct a risk matrix that communicates to executives
Create a prioritized remediation roadmap for LoneStar DevOps

callouts.text: This is Week 2 of a 3-week module. Save all work to your Lab5-Screenshots folder. You’ll compile these into your Lab 5.3 Risk & Vulnerability Assessment Report next week.

callouts.title: Analysis-Focused Lab
callouts.text: Lab 5.1 completion is required. You need your vulnerability findings from last week — the 28 vulnerabilities you identified. This lab is analysis-focused rather than tool-focused. You won’t be running terminal commands — you’ll be working with CVSS calculators in your browser and building tables and matrices. Your rationale matters more than your ranking.

timeGuide.timesheetNote: This is Week 2 of a 3-week module. Log as Internal Training: 3–4 hours — Risk assessment and prioritization. This is analysis-focused work, not tool-running — your CVSS rationale and business context notes are the deliverable. Save all screenshots (#11–18) to your existing Lab53-Screenshots folder.

content.whatYoureDoing: Scoring each vulnerability from Lab 5.1 using the CVSS 3.1 calculator.
content.whyItMatters: CVSS provides a standardized way to communicate vulnerability severity. It’s the common language between security teams, vendors, and clients.

steps.text: Access the CVSS Calculator: NVD calculator at nvd.nist.gov/vuln-metrics/cvss/v3-calculator or FIRST calculator at first.org/cvss/calculator/3.1
For each vulnerability: look up the CVE (if available) or calculate based on characteristics
Document components: Attack Vector, Attack Complexity, Privileges Required, User Interaction, Scope, C/I/A Impact
Create a comprehensive table: CVE ID (if applicable), Vulnerability Name, Base Score, Severity Rating

content.description: Capture CVSS calculator with one vulnerability scored.
Capture your complete vulnerability scores table.

items.label: Confusing Attack Vector with Attack Complexity
items.text: Attack Vector is where the attacker must be (network, adjacent, local, physical). Attack Complexity is how hard the exploit is to execute.

items.label: Using CVSS 2.0 scores
items.text: Some older CVE entries show CVSS 2.0 scores. Always use the v3.1 calculator at first.org for consistency.

items.label: Skipping the justification
items.text: A CVSS score without explanation is meaningless — document why you selected each metric value.

content.text: What’s the difference between CVSS Base, Temporal, and Environmental scores? Research how different organizations use each type.
You just researched CVSS scoring. Add it to your references: FIRST. Common Vulnerability Scoring System v3.1: Specification Document. https://www.first.org/cvss/specification-document
A complete table with CVSS scores for all vulnerabilities identified in Lab 5.1.
See “Vulnerability Assessment” section.

content.whatYoureDoing: Creating a vulnerability-to-asset mapping that shows how each finding affects LoneStar’s business.
content.whyItMatters: A CVSS 9.8 on a test server matters less than a CVSS 7.3 on a production database holding customer secrets. Business context determines actual risk.

steps.text: Identify critical assets: webapp-prod (customer portal), db-primary (MySQL with customer data), dev-api (Node.js), mgmt-ssh (management access)
Create vulnerability-to-asset mapping: which asset(s) does each vulnerability affect?
Assess impact categories: Financial, Reputational, Operational, Compliance
Document business context: $12M ARR, 200+ customers, Series B preparation

content.title: CEO & Co-Founder, LoneStar DevOps
content.quote: I need to present to Longhorn Ventures in three weeks. I need to know: what could actually hurt us, and what’s the plan to fix it?

content.title: Don’t Forget jdavis
content.text: Former employee with sudo access is a critical business risk — left 6 months ago, works for competitor.

items.label: Technical-only focus
items.text: A “medium” vuln on a critical asset may need higher priority.

items.label: Missing context
items.text: Document WHY each asset matters to the business.

items.label: Forgetting dependencies
items.text: A compromised build server affects everything it deploys.

content.text: How do you calculate the business impact of a potential breach? Research qualitative vs. quantitative risk assessment methods.
You just researched business impact. Add it to your references: FAIR: Factor Analysis of Information Risk. The Open Group, https://www.opengroup.org/certifications/openfair
A mapping of vulnerabilities to business assets with documented business impact.
See “Business Impact Analysis” section.

content.whatYoureDoing: Building a 5×5 risk matrix plotting vulnerabilities by likelihood and impact.
content.whyItMatters: Executives understand “red quadrant” better than “CVSS 9.8.” A risk matrix translates technical assessments into business language.

steps.text: Define Likelihood Levels (1–5): Rare → Unlikely → Possible → Likely → Almost Certain
Define Impact Levels (1–5): Negligible → Minor → Moderate → Major → Catastrophic
Calculate Risk Scores: Risk = Likelihood × Impact. Critical (15–25), High (10–14), Medium (5–9), Low (1–4)
Create Visual Matrix: 5×5 grid, color-coded by risk level

items.label: Inconsistent scales
items.text: Use the same 1–5 scale for both likelihood and impact.

items.label: Missing color coding
items.text: Visual distinction makes the matrix actionable.

items.label: No thresholds defined
items.text: Document what score ranges mean (critical, high, medium, low).

content.text: What is defense in depth and how does it relate to vulnerability management? Research layered security controls and compensating controls.
You just researched defense strategies. Add it to your references: CIS Controls v8. Center for Internet Security, https://www.cisecurity.org/controls
A complete risk matrix with all vulnerabilities categorized.
See “Risk Assessment Frameworks” section.

content.whatYoureDoing: Creating a prioritized remediation roadmap that considers real-world constraints.
content.whyItMatters: A report that says ‘fix everything immediately’ is useless. LoneStar has a 2-person DevOps team, no staging environment, and can’t take the portal offline during business hours.

content.text: What factors beyond CVSS scores should influence vulnerability prioritization? Research how business context affects remediation urgency.
FIRST Stakeholder-Specific Vulnerability Categorization (SSVC): https://www.first.org/cvss/. Your textbook Chapter 10 covers business impact analysis.

steps.text: Document constraints: 2 DevOps engineers (Sarah + Miguel), no staging, no business-hours downtime, 4-week timeline
Assess remediation complexity: effort, downtime, skill level, testing requirements
Create prioritization matrix: Risk Score vs. Remediation Complexity
Build prioritized roadmap: sequenced list with rationale for each position

content.title: Senior DevOps Engineer, LoneStar DevOps
content.quote: It’s just me and Miguel, and we can’t flip a switch and fix everything overnight. Give me quick wins I can show Amanda next week, and a realistic plan for the harder stuff.

content.title: Key Insight: CVSS vs. Business Priority
content.text: WordPress SQLi (CVSS 9.8) = High priority (marketing site). MySQL default creds (~CVSS 7.5) = HIGHEST priority (exposes ALL customer data).

content.description: Capture final remediation roadmap with rationale.

items.label: Unrealistic timelines
items.text: A 2-person team can’t fix 28 vulns in one sprint.

items.label: No quick wins
items.text: Include some easy fixes to show early progress.

items.label: Missing rationale
items.text: Explain WHY you prioritized each item.

content.text: How do you communicate risk to non-technical stakeholders? Research executive risk reporting and how to translate technical findings into business language.
You just researched risk communication. Add it to your references: ISACA Risk IT Framework. ISACA, https://www.isaca.org/resources/risk-it
A prioritized remediation roadmap that accounts for LoneStar’s constraints with clear rationale.
See “Risk Treatment” section.

items.text: Attackers don’t prioritize by CVSS — they prioritize by attack path. MySQL default creds → customer database → API keys → cascade to 200+ customer environments.
Defenders need to communicate risk to stakeholders who don’t understand CVEs. Your deliverables need to serve both technical and business audiences.

purpleTeam.connection: Effective risk assessment combines both perspectives: technically sound + practically implementable.

screenshotTable.description: CVSS 3.1 calculator with one vulnerability scored
Complete table of all vulnerabilities with CVSS scores
Business impact assessment for LoneStar assets
Completed 5×5 risk matrix with color coding
Prioritization framework (Risk Score vs. Remediation Complexity)
Final remediation roadmap with phased rationale

sections.title: Checkpoint Verification
items: All Lab 5.1 vulnerabilities have CVSS scores
5×5 risk matrix is complete and color-coded
Prioritized remediation roadmap created with rationale

canvasSubmission.text: Take the Lab 5.2 Quiz in Canvas.

channels.label: Discord
channels.detail: Post in #lab-help for technical issues (fastest response)

needHelp.lateWorkPolicy: Late submissions are not accepted. The course drop policies exist for emergencies — use them wisely. This lab is eligible to be dropped, but don’t use that as an excuse to skip foundational work.

permitted: Explain command syntax and tool functionality
prohibited: Complete the hands-on work for you
genAI.principle: Think of AI like a tutor, not a ghostwriter. It can help you understand and improve, but the work itself must be yours.

whatsNext.title: Lab 5.3: Risk & Vulnerability Assessment Report
whatsNext.text: Compile everything from Weeks 1 and 2 into a professional assessment report for LoneStar DevOps. Amanda needs a report for investors; Sarah needs a roadmap for her team.
